summary:
The Protestant Reformation was a sweeping religious, intellectual, and political transformation of 16th-century Europe that permanently fractured the unity of Western Christianity, producing dozens of new Protestant denominations and reshaping the political map of Europe. Sparked by [[event:2430|Martin Luther's 95 Theses]] (1517), the movement quickly spread beyond theological disputes about indulgences and church authority to challenge the entire institutional structure of the Catholic Church, its sacramental system, its monopoly on biblical interpretation, and the pope's spiritual and temporal authority. [[event:2431|John Calvin's]] theology in Geneva, [[event:2432|Henry VIII's]] break with Rome, Ulrich Zwingli's reformation in Zurich, and the Anabaptist radical wing all added distinct streams to the Protestant movement that made any unified counter-response difficult.

The Reformation's consequences extended far beyond theology. The [[event:2328|Thirty Years' War]], the [[event:2327|English Civil War]], the [[event:2406|Dutch Revolt]], and scores of regional conflicts were fought along confessional lines. The [[event:2322|Council of Trent]] and the [[event:2433|Jesuit order]] represented the Catholic Church's Counter-Reformation response, which both reformed internal abuses and sharpened Catholic doctrinal distinctiveness. Politically, the [[event:2493|Peace of Augsburg]] (1555) and later the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]] (1648) enshrined the principle that princes determined their territories' religion (cuius regio, eius religio), embedding confessional difference into European state structure. Intellectually, the Reformation's emphasis on individual Bible-reading drove literacy campaigns, vernacular translation, and the spread of the [[event:3834|printing press]], contributing to the broader cultural transformation of the [[event:96|Renaissance]] into modernity.
